content: Nahum the prophet was from where?
explanation: Nahum 1:1 — 'An oracle concerning Nineveh. The book of the vision of Nahum of Elkosh.' Elkosh is likely a village in Judah.

content: The book of Nahum is an oracle concerning which city?
explanation: Nahum 1:1 — 'An oracle concerning Nineveh.' The entire book announces the LORD's judgment against Nineveh, the capital of Assyria.

content: According to Nahum 1:2, what kind of God is the LORD?
explanation: Nahum 1:2 — 'The LORD is a jealous and avenging God; the LORD is avenging and wrathful...' His jealousy (qannâ') is a holy jealousy, guarding His holiness and His people.
options: A God who is only absolutely merciful
A God who always overlooks all sin

content: According to Nahum 1:3, though slow to anger, what great attribute does the LORD have?
explanation: Nahum 1:3 — 'The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, and the LORD will by no means clear the guilty.' Slow to anger does not mean indifferent to evil — He remains mighty in judgment.
tags: Slow to anger, great in power

content: According to Nahum 1:3, where is the way of the LORD?
explanation: Nahum 1:3 — '...His way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet.' A majestic theophany — storms mark the advent of God.
options: In the depths of the sea

content: According to Nahum 1:7, in what day is the LORD a stronghold?
explanation: Nahum 1:7 — 'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him.' The same God who judges Nineveh is refuge for those who trust Him.
options: The first day of the month

content: According to Nahum 1:7, whom does the LORD know?
explanation: Nahum 1:7 — '...he knows those who take refuge in him.' 'Knows' here means intimate relational care — the LORD tenderly attends to those who trust Him.
options: The highly learned of the people
Those who take refuge in Him

content: According to Nahum 1:15, upon the mountains, what does the messenger bring that is celebrated?
explanation: Nahum 1:15 — 'Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace!' This echoes Isaiah 52:7 — joyful news of deliverance when Nineveh falls.

content: In Nahum 2:1, who has come up against Nineveh?
explanation: Nahum 2:1 — 'The scatterer has come up against you. Man the ramparts; watch the road...' The Scatterer foretells the Medo-Babylonian coalition that destroyed Nineveh in 612 BC.

content: Nahum 2:13 declares 'I am against you' — who speaks this?
explanation: Nahum 2:13 — 'Behold, I am against you, declares the LORD of hosts, and I will burn your chariots in smoke...' 'LORD of hosts' emphasizes command over the heavenly armies.

content: According to Nahum 3:1, how is Nineveh described?
explanation: Nahum 3:1 — 'Woe to the bloody city, all full of lies and plunder — no end to the prey!' 'Bloody city' reflects Assyria's notorious cruelty.
options: The bloody city, full of lies and plunder

content: According to Nahum 3:7, when Nineveh falls, who will grieve for her?
explanation: Nahum 3:7 — '...Nineveh is laid waste; who will grieve for her? Where shall I seek comforters for you?' None will mourn — her crimes were so great that her fall brings relief.
options: No one — no comforters will be found

content: According to Nahum 3:8, Nineveh is compared to which previously fallen city?
explanation: Nahum 3:8 — 'Are you better than Thebes that sat by the Nile, with water around her...' Thebes (No-Amon) of Egypt was sacked by Assyria itself in 663 BC.

content: Nahum 3:19 says all who hear news of Nineveh will do what?
explanation: Nahum 3:19 — '...all who hear the news about you clap their hands over you. For upon whom has not come your unceasing evil?' Clapping signals relief and rejoicing that the wicked city has fallen.

content: According to Nahum 1:8, by what does the LORD make a complete end of His adversaries?
explanation: Nahum 1:8 — 'But with an overflowing flood he will make a complete end of the adversaries, and will pursue his enemies into darkness.' This may have been fulfilled when the Tigris River overflowed and breached Nineveh's walls.

content: According to Nahum 2:6, when the river gates are opened, what happens?
explanation: Nahum 2:6 — 'The river gates are opened; the palace melts away.' When waters rush in, the royal palace of Nineveh collapses — fulfilled in the fall of 612 BC.

content: Nahum 2:11 asks, 'Where is the lions' den?' — this image refers to which nation?
explanation: Nahum 2:11-12 — 'Where is the lions' den, the feeding place of the young lions... The lion tore enough for his cubs...' The lion was the royal symbol of Assyria; now its 'den' is abandoned as the empire falls.

content: According to Nahum 1:2-3, which attributes are ascribed to the LORD? (select all that apply)
explanation: Nahum 1:2-3 — 'The LORD is a jealous and avenging God... The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, and the LORD will by no means clear the guilty.' Three clear attributes; 'forgets all sins' is contrary — He does not clear the guilty.
options: Forgets all the sins of the wicked

content: According to Nahum 3:1, which traits describe Nineveh? (select all that apply)
explanation: Nahum 3:1 — 'Woe to the bloody city, all full of lies and plunder — no end to the prey!' Three traits; 'beacon of righteousness' contradicts the whole indictment.
options: Full of plunder — no end to the prey
Beacon of righteousness to the nations

content: Nahum 1:7 describes the LORD with which attributes? (select all that apply)
explanation: Nahum 1:7 — 'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him.' Three positive descriptions; 'never sees evil' contradicts the rest of the book.
options: A stronghold in the day of trouble
He knows those who take refuge in Him
